title: Blake Lively's Lawsuit Against Justin Baldoni: What Happened in Court
summary: A judge has dismissed most of the claims in a lawsuit filed by actress Blake Lively against her co-star Justin Baldoni. The lawsuit started in 2024 when Lively accused Baldoni of harassment and trying to ruin her reputation while they worked together on a movie called It Ends with Us. The judge threw out 10 out of 13 allegations, including harassment and defamation, but kept three claims: breach of contract, retaliation, and aiding in retaliation. These three claims will go to a trial in New York in May. Lively's lawyer says the case is about retaliation and damage to her reputation, not just harassment. Baldoni denied the claims and had his own lawsuit against Lively dismissed last year. The case involves their interactions on the movie set, where Lively played a character named Lily Bloom.

background_read: This case involves a legal dispute between two actors from Hollywood. Blake Lively is a famous actress known for roles in movies and TV shows, and Justin Baldoni is an actor and director. They worked together on a movie based on a popular book. Lawsuits are common in Hollywood when people have disagreements, and courts help decide who is right. Understanding terms like lawsuit, judge, and trial can help kids learn how the legal system works to solve problems fairly.
Article_Structure: WHO: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni, actors in a movie, with Judge Lewis Liman making the decision.
WHAT: A judge dismissed most claims in a lawsuit about harassment and reputation damage, leaving three claims for trial.
WHERE: The case is in New York, where the trial will happen.
WHY: The lawsuit started because Lively accused Baldoni of bad behavior on set, and the judge ruled based on legal reasons.

perspectives.description: She believes Baldoni harassed her and retaliated to ruin her reputation, and she wants to prove this in court.
He denies the allegations and says Lively's claims are false, as shown by his counter-suit being dismissed.
